sections.header: Verses 1–9 — “They Return at Evening, Snarling Like Dogs”

heb.paragraph: "They return at evening, snarling like dogs (kəlābîm), and prowl about the city" (v.6). Written when Saul sent men to watch David’s house to kill him (1 Sam 19:11). The enemies are compared to prowling dogs — scavengers circling in the dark. The refrain: the dogs return, snarl, prowl (vv.6, 14). David asks God to defend him not because of his own merit but because of the enemies’ wickedness.

notes.note: "Deliver me from my enemies. Protect me from those who rise up against me." The siege situation: assassins outside, David trapped inside.
"They return at evening, snarling like dogs. See what they spew from their mouths — swords are on their lips." The dog-pack image: nocturnal, menacing, feral. Their speech is weaponised.
"But you laugh at them, LORD; you scoff at all those nations." God’s response to the threat: laughter (echoing Psalm 2:4). What terrifies David amuses God.

refs.note: Paul's escape from Damascus when enemies watched the gates parallels David's situation with Saul's men.
Paul lowered in a basket recalls the same surrounded-but-delivered pattern the psalm describes.
Rulers conspiring against Jesus echoes the psalm's enemies plotting bloodshed.

hist.context: Psalm 59 is attributed to David when Saul sent men to watch his house to kill him (1 Sam 19:11-17). Michal helped David escape through a window. The repeated 'howling like dogs' refrain (vv.6, 14) portrays enemies as scavengers prowling the city at night. The 'Do Not Destroy' superscription links this psalm with 57-58.

sections.header: Verses 10–17 — “God Is My Fortress, My God of Unfailing Love”

heb.paragraph: "You are my fortress (misgabbî), my refuge in times of trouble" (v.16). David asks God not to kill the enemies immediately but to scatter them as a lesson (vv.11–12). Let them wander and be consumed (v.13). The refrain returns: the dogs prowl (v.14), but David sings of God’s strength in the morning (v.16). "I will sing of your love; for you are my fortress, my refuge in times of trouble" (v.16).

notes.note: "Do not kill them, Lord, or my people will forget; scatter them by your power." A remarkable request: don’t destroy them instantly — let their wandering be a visible lesson. Memory requires visible evidence.

"I will sing of your strength, in the morning I will sing of your love. For you are my fortress, my refuge in times of trouble." Morning follows the night of the prowling dogs. David’s song replaces their snarling.

refs.note: God as refuge and strength is the same fortress theology this psalm celebrates.
David's song calling God his fortress, refuge, and stronghold develops the same vocabulary.
The name of the LORD as a strong tower echoes the psalm's fortress imagery.

hist.context: The prayer that God not kill enemies but 'scatter them' and 'bring them down' (v.11) asks for prolonged humiliation rather than swift destruction — enemies should serve as living warnings. The concluding praise (vv.16-17) with singing and morning celebration suggests liturgical use in dawn worship services following dangerous nights.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Besieged in his own home by Saul's assassins (superscription, vv.1-5,9-10,16-17)
ppl.text: The superscription refers to 1 Samuel 19:11, when Saul sent men to David's house to kill him and Michal lowered David through a window. David describes enemies who 'return at evening, snarling like dogs, and prowl about the city' (vv.6,14) — a repeated refrain painting his assassins as feral pack animals. Despite mortal danger, David's response is worship: 'I will sing of your strength, in the morning I will sing of your love' (v.16). Morning worship follows a night of siege — survival itself becomes a hymn.

ppl.name: God (Yahweh Elohim Tsevaot)
ppl.role: Fortress-God who laughs at conspiring nations (vv.5,8-9,11,17)
ppl.text: David piles up divine titles: 'LORD God Almighty, God of Israel' (v.5). God 'laughs at them' and 'scoffs at all those nations' (v.8) — divine derision echoing Psalm 2:4. David calls God 'my fortress' (misgav, vv.9,16,17) three times — the military metaphor for an impregnable high position. Remarkably, David asks God not to kill his enemies immediately but to 'scatter them by your power and bring them down' as a lasting lesson (v.11) — prolonged exposure of evil serves as public testimony to God's justice.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVSee how they lie in wait for me! Fierce men conspire against me for no offense or sin of mine, Lord.ESVFor behold, they lie in wait for my life; fierce men stir up strife against me. For no transgression or sin of mine, O LORD,NIVSee what they spew from their mouths— the words from their lips are sharp as swords, and they think, “Who can hear us?”ESVThere they are, bellowing with their mouths with swords in their lips\u2014 for \u201cWho,\u201d they think, \u201cwill hear us?\u201d

rec.text: ""Deliver me from my enemies. Protect me from those who rise up against me." The siege situation: assassins outside, David trapped inside."

rows.label: The Dog Pack — 59:1–9
rows.text: Snarling dogs prowl at evening. Assassins surround the house. "But you laugh at them."

hebtext.note: "They return at evening, snarling like dogs (kəlābîm), and prowl about the city" (v.6). Written when Saul sent men to watch David’s house to kill him (1 Sam 19:11). The enemies are compared to prowling dogs — scavengers circling in the dark. The refrain: the dogs return, snarl, prowl (vv.6, 14). David asks God to defend him not because of his own merit but because of the enemies’ wickedness.

"You are my fortress (misgabbî), my refuge in times of trouble" (v.16). David asks God not to kill the enemies immediately but to scatter them as a lesson (vv.11–12). Let them wander and be consumed (v.13). The refrain returns: the dogs prowl (v.14), but David sings of God’s strength in the morning (v.16). "I will sing of your love; for you are my fortress, my refuge in times of trouble" (v.16).

themes.note: Worship: morning song replaces evening snarling. Sovereignty: God laughs at the assassins. Faith: fortress and refuge in trouble.
